text: Hello, this is Dr. Badrugia. This video is to show a robotic cholecystectomy that I performed.
Specifically, the video is to show the ICG and Firefly mode. on the robotic platform, specifically with the DaVinci XI system.
The purpose of the video was to answer the question by various staff members of why I use ICG and the Firefly mode, and I wanted to show what exactly I see.
The robotic system is docked, and the instruments are positioned at the start of the procedure. The gallbladder is visible in the surgical field.
The liver, initially appearing brown, is shown turning green under the ICG and Firefly mode on the robotic platform.
The surgeon begins the dissection at the top of the gallbladder using the dome down technique, visible in the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a created window to lift the gallbladder, providing better exposure for further dissection.

The video shows acute cholecystitis with visible edema and fluid accumulation between the liver and gallbladder. The dissection is performed at an accelerated speed, highlighting the separation of these structures.
The gallbladder is closely attached to the liver, with visible use of ICG dye and Firefly mode enhancing the visualization of anatomical structures.
The Firefly mode is activated, revealing the liver in a bright green color due to the laser-based visualization of the intravenously administered dye processed by the liver.

After about half an hour or so, the dye gets into the bile ducts and then is excreted out through the bile into the intestines. And so timing of this is somewhat important. You don't want to give it too soon before the procedure and you want to give enough time for the dye to get into the liver and then into the bile ducts and subsequently where you'd be able to see it.
The surgeon continues the procedure under regular lighting, using a harmonic scalpel in the right hand to perform the dissection. There is a visible minor bleeding in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument on the left arm for electrocautery, while a third instrument retracts the liver upwards. As dissection progresses, the liver is gradually elevated to improve exposure.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver, revealing scarred tissue indicative of acute on chronic cholecystitis.
In Firefly mode, the liver appears bright green due to the laser-based visualization of the dye. The gallbladder, affected by acute cholecystitis, does not exhibit the same brightness as the liver.
The gallbladder appears gray and non-colored due to a presumed stone blocking bile flow, indicating acute cholecystitis. In contrast, the liver is bright green, highlighted by the ICG dye.
The surgeon uses the Firefly mode to enhance visualization of the interface between the gallbladder and the liver, continuing to dissect and free the gallbladder from the liver tissue.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the neck of the gallbladder, approaching the area where the cystic duct and artery are located. The pace of dissection slows to better visualize the anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to achieve hemostasis, controlling minor bleeding in the surgical field. The slower mode allows for better visualization of the nuanced tissue interactions during the procedure.

The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder from the liver while retracting the liver upwards. Yellow fatty tissue is visible, and the green coloration indicates the common bile duct running vertically.

In Firefly mode, the surgeon highlights the neck of the gallbladder, where minimal green dye is visible, indicating limited ICG uptake due to obstruction. The surrounding liver tissue appears brightly lit and green.

The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder from the liver, with the instrument revealing green dye within the gallbladder and cystic duct. The visualization mode helps distinguish safe areas for dissection.

The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder, revealing more of the anatomical structures. The video shows a clear Y configuration with dye visible within the gallbladder above the instrument. To the right of the instrument, the cystic duct is seen joining the common bile duct.

The surgeon switches between regular and Firefly mode, highlighting the cystic duct, which is more easily visible in Firefly mode. The dissection continues to free the cystic duct from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon navigates around the cystic duct, with the gallbladder visible to the left and fatty tissue being freed. The cystic duct is clearly seen, while the common bile duct is on the right side, extending upwards into the liver and downwards towards the intestines.
The surgeon observes green dye in the duodenum and antrum, indicating bile presence in the stomach and intestines.

The surgeon uses a clip applier to place two permanent clips on the cystic duct. After securing the clips, the same instrument is used to divide the cystic duct, completing this step of the procedure.
You can see the anatomy very nicely. Again, this is DarkBadRuja. Thank you for watching this video.
